display_name: Cannot reach a hostname usually means DNS resolution failed, not that the routing path is broken
context: Operator reports connectivity failure to a hostname, or a feature that depends on DNS resolution (NTP server by name, syslog server by name, TACACS by name) is not working
observation: Pings or connections to a hostname fail; the operator assumes the destination is unreachable or the routing path is broken
naive_interpretation: There is a routing or connectivity problem between the device and the destination — check routes, interfaces, and ACLs along the path
correct_interpretation: When a hostname-based operation fails, the first thing to verify is whether the hostname resolves to an IP address at all. Network devices depend on DNS resolution working correctly — if the configured name-server is unreachable, misconfigured, or the DNS record does not exist, every hostname-based operation fails regardless of whether the IP path is functional. Ping the destination by IP address first. If that works, the problem is DNS, not routing. Common causes: no name-server configured, name-server unreachable from the VRF the device is using, DNS server not responding (firewall blocking UDP/53 or TCP/53), stale DNS cache, or the hostname simply has no A/AAAA record. Many network devices do not cache DNS aggressively, so intermittent DNS server availability causes intermittent hostname reachability that looks exactly like flapping connectivity.
why: Network devices resolve hostnames at the time of use, not at configuration time — if DNS fails at query time, the operation fails even if it worked before
The name-server used by the device may be in a different VRF than the management interface, or the DNS source interface may not be configured, causing the DNS query itself to fail at routing
Many operators configure NTP, syslog, TACACS/RADIUS servers by hostname — a single DNS outage breaks all of these simultaneously, which looks like a widespread network problem
Ping by hostname invokes DNS resolution before sending any ICMP — the 'destination unreachable' or timeout error is from the DNS lookup, not from the ICMP path

confirmation_commands.purpose: Display configured name-servers and cached DNS entries to verify DNS is reachable and the hostname has a cached resolution
Display DNS configuration, name-servers, and cached hostname-to-IP mappings
Ping the destination by IP to confirm routing works independently of DNS resolution
Verify name-server is configured and reachable from the correct VRF
Display configured DNS servers and verify they are reachable

traps: Immediately checking routing tables and interface states when a hostname-based connection fails, wasting time on a path that may be perfectly functional
Not realizing that the device's DNS source interface or VRF context differs from the data-plane path — DNS can fail while IP forwarding works fine
Treating intermittent hostname reachability as a routing flap when it is actually intermittent DNS server availability

Configuring management services (NTP, syslog, TACACS) by hostname and then losing all of them simultaneously when the DNS server becomes unreachable — this looks like a major outage but has a single-point cause
Forgetting that some platforms do not enable DNS resolution by default — 'no ip domain-lookup' on IOS/IOS-XE silently prevents all hostname resolution
